blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XII
QUADRICSby P. DELIGNE
0.0. The present exposé gathers together some well-known results on quadrics, which we will need in order to prove the Picard- Lefschetz formula.

0.1. Let \(m\) be a section of an \(\mathcal O_S\)-Module \(M\) on a scheme \(S\). We will say that \(m\) is everywhere nonzero on \(S\) if, for every \(s\in S\), the image of \(m\) in the \(k(s)\)-vector space \(M_s\) deduced from \(M\) by the base change \(\{s\}\longrightarrow S\) is nonzero. The same terminology applies for \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and \(m\) an element of an \(A\)-module \(M\).
1. Quadratic forms

1.1. Let \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) be an affine scheme, \(V\) a locally free \(A\)-module of rank \(n\) and \(Q\) a quadratic form on \(V\) (Bourb. Alg 9 § 3 n°4). Recall that \(Q\) is said to be non-degenerate if the associated bilinear form
is non-degenerate, i.e. establishes an isomorphism between \(V\) and its dual \(V^*\). If 2 is not invertible in \(A\), this can only happen for \(n\) even (loc. cit.).
The quadratic forms on \(V\) form a projective \(A\)-module; a quadratic form \(Q\) is "everywhere nonzero" on \(S\) (0.1) if and only if the \(Q(v)\) generate the ideal \(A\) of \(A\).

The notation \(\mathbb P(V)\) will be taken in the sense of EGA: recall that if \(A\) is a field, then \(\mathbb P(V)\) is the projective space whose set of rational points is the quotient \((V^*-\{0\})/A^*\).

The quadratic form \(Q\) is identified with a section of the invertible sheaf \(\mathcal O(2)\) on the projective space \(\mathbb P(V^*)\) over \(S\). For \(Q\) everywhere nonzero, the ("homogeneous") equation \(Q=0\) defines a subscheme of \(\mathbb P(V^*)\), flat and purely of relative dimension \(n-2\) over \(S\). This scheme is called the quadric defined by \(Q\). The form \(Q\) is said to be ordinary if it vanishes at no point of \(S\) and the quadric it defines is smooth over \(S\).
In order that \(Q\) be ordinary, it is necessary and sufficient that the forms deduced from it after extension of scalars from \(A\) to a field be so. If \(A\) is a field, then:

1. a) for \(n\) even or \(\operatorname{car}(A)\ne2\): \(Q\) ordinary \(\Longleftrightarrow Q\) non-degenerate;
  2. b) for \(n\) odd and \(\operatorname{car}(A)=2\): \(Q\) is ordinary if and only if the kernel \(N\) of the associated (alternating) bilinear form \(\Phi\) is of dimension one, and \(Q\) is not zero on \(N\).

Proposition 1.2. If \(Q\) is ordinary and if \(n=2m\) (resp. \(n=2m+1\)), then, locally for the étale topology on \(S\), \(V\) admits a basis \(e\) such that
\[ Q\left(\sum_1^n x_i e_i\right)=\sum_1^m x_i x_{i+m} \]

(resp.

\[ Q\left(\sum_1^n x_i e_i\right)=\sum_1^m x_i x_{i+m}+\lambda x_{2m+1}^2\quad(\lambda\text{ invertible}). \]

)

Let us prove 1.2 by induction on \(m\). If \(m=0\), the assertion is obvious. If \(m>0\), the quadric with equation \(Q=0\) is smooth over \(S\) and has nonempty geometric fibers; it therefore admits sections locally for the étale topology. Locally, such a section is defined by an element \(e\in V\), vanishing at no point of \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and such that \(Q(e)=0\). At no point of \(S\) is \(e\) in the kernel of \(\Phi\); locally, we can therefore find \(f'\) such that \(\Phi(e,f')=1\). If \(f=-Q(f')e+f'\), we have
If \(V_2\) is the orthogonal complement of the submodule \(V_1\) of \(V\) generated by \(e\) and \(f\), we have \(V=V_1\oplus V_2\), and we conclude by applying the induction hypothesis to \(V_2\).
1.3. For the definition and the main properties of the Clifford algebra of \(Q\), we refer to (Bourb. Alg 9 § 9). We denote by \(\rho\) the canonical map from \(V\) to \(C(Q)\).

Lemma 1.3.1. Under the hypotheses of 1.1, there exists for \(\lambda\in A\) one and only one homomorphism
\[ [\lambda]:C^+(\lambda Q)\longrightarrow C^+(Q) \]

such that, for \(x,y\in V\), we have

\[ [\lambda]\ (\rho(x).\rho(y))=\lambda.\rho(x).\rho(y). \]
This homomorphism is deduced by passage to the quotient from the endomorphism \([\lambda]\) of the part \(T^+(E)\) of the tensor algebra of \(E\), which equals \(\lambda^n\) on \(T^{2n}(E)\). Indeed,
\[ [\lambda]\,(x\otimes x-\lambda Q(x))=\lambda(x\otimes x-Q(x)). \]
Suppose \(Q\) everywhere nonzero on \(S\). Then, lemma 1.2.1 defines a transitive system of isomorphisms between the algebras \(C^+(\lambda Q)\) for \(\lambda\in A^*\).

Consequently, the algebra \(C^+(Q)\) depends (up to unique isomorphism) only on \(V\) and on the datum of \(Q\) up to a factor; in other words, \(C^+(Q)\) depends only on \(V\) and on the subscheme with equation \(Q=0\) of \(\mathbb P(V^*)\). Every homothety with invertible ratio is an automorphism of the pair \((V,X\subset\mathbb P(V^*))\); one checks that these automorphisms act trivially on \(C^+(V,X)\), and it follows that the algebra \(C^+(V,X)\) depends only on the pair of schemes \(X\subset\mathbb P(V^*)\) over \(A\). We will denote it \(C^+(X,\mathbb P(V^*))\).

1.4. Let \(V\) be a locally free \(A\)-module of rank \(2m>0\) and \(Q\) a non-degenerate quadratic form on \(V\). Suppose given a decomposition \(V=W_1\oplus W_2\) of \(V\) into two totally singular subspaces. The form \(Q(w_1+w_2)\) then puts \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) in perfect duality. Recall that for \(f\in W_2\) (identified with \(W_1^*\)), the contracted product by \(f\) is the endomorphism of degree \(-1\) of \(\bigwedge W_1\) defined by
\[ f\mathbin{\lrcorner}(e_0\wedge\cdots\wedge e_p) =\sum(-1)^i\langle e_i,f\rangle\,e_0\wedge\cdots\wedge\widehat e_i\wedge\cdots\wedge e_p. \]
Let us equip the \(A\)-module \(\bigwedge W_1\) with the \(\mathbb Z/2\)-grading for which
\[ (\bigwedge W_1)^+=\bigoplus_i\bigwedge^{2i}W_1 \quad\text{and}\quad (\bigwedge W_1)^-=\bigoplus_i\bigwedge^{2i+1}W_1. \]
The algebra \(\operatorname{End}(\bigwedge W_1)\) is then \(\mathbb Z/2\)-graded: the even endomorphisms (resp. odd) are those which respect the grading (resp. are of degree 1 (mod 2)).
We refer to (Bourb Alg 9 § 9 n°4) for the proof of the existence of a unique isomorphism of \(\mathbb Z/2\)-graded algebras \(s\) between \(C(Q)\) and \(\operatorname{End}(\bigwedge W_1)\), such that
\[ s(\rho((e,0)))=e\wedge\qquad\text{and}\qquad s(\rho((0,f)))=f\mathbin{\lrcorner}. \]

Let us provisionally denote by \(e(W_1,W_2)\) the element of \(C^+(Q)\) such that \(s(e(W_1,W_2))\) is the identity on \((\bigwedge W_1)^+\) and 0 on \((\bigwedge W_1)^-\). The center \(Z(Q)\) of \(C^+(Q)\) is isomorphic to \(A\oplus A\), this decomposition being defined by the idempotents \(e(W_1,W_2)\) and \(1-e(W_1,W_2)\). Moreover \(C^+(Q)\) is a matrix algebra over \(Z(Q)\).
From this and from 1.2, we deduce as in loc. cit.,

Proposition 1.5. Under the hypotheses of 1.1, if \(n=2m>0\) and \(Q\) is non-degenerate, then the center \(Z(Q)\) of \(C^+(Q)\) is an étale algebra, locally free of rank 2 over \(A\), and \(C^+(Q)\) is an Azumaya algebra over \(Z(Q)\).

1.6. Let \(V\) and \(Q\) be as in 1.4, and \(W\) a totally singular direct factor, locally free of rank \(m\), of \(V\). Let \(W'_1\) be a complement of \(W\): \(V=W\oplus W'_1\). Every complement \(W'_2\) of \(W\) is identified with the graph of a map \(u\) from \(W'_1\) to \(W\). The form \(\Phi\) puts \(W\) and \(W'_1\) in duality; the map \(u\) is thus uniquely determined by the bilinear form on \(W'_1\)
and \(W'_2\) is totally singular if and only if, on \(W'_1\), we have

Since such forms \(B\) always exist, \(W\) admits totally singular complements. One of them, say \(W'\), being chosen, the set of totally singular complements \(W'_0\) of \(W\) is identified with the set of alternating forms on \(W'\), or again with the set of sections of the affine space \(\mathbb E(\bigwedge^2 W')\) over \(S\). For each section \(x\) of \(\mathbb E(\bigwedge^2 W')\), let \(W'_0(x)\) be the corresponding complement of \(W\) and \(e(x)\) the idempotent \(e(W,W'_0(x))\). The function \(x\longmapsto e(x)\) is compatible with any extension of scalars, hence defines a morphism of \(S\)-schemes \(e\) from \(\mathbb E(\bigwedge^2 W')\) to \(\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q))\). The \(S\)-scheme \(\mathbb E(\bigwedge^2 W')\) is flat with connected geometric fibers, whereas \(\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q))\) is étale over \(S\). The morphism \(e\) therefore factors through a section of \(\mathbb E(\bigwedge^2 W')\) over \(S\), i.e. \(e(x)\) is independent of \(x\). This justifies the
Notation 1.7. For \(W\) as above and \(W'\) any totally isotropic complement of \(W\), we denote by \(e(W)\) the idempotent \(e(W,W')\in Z(Q)\).
1.8. We saw in 1.6 that, for \(\lambda\) invertible, \(C^+(Q)\) is identified with \(C^+(\lambda Q)\). A continuity argument analogous to the preceding one shows that this identification transforms
\[ e(W)\in Z(Q)\quad\text{into}\quad e(W)\in Z(\lambda Q). \]
1.9. Recall that if \(B\) and \(C\) are two \(\mathbb Z/2\)-graded algebras, then, in the graded tensor product \(B\otimes' C\) of \(B\) and \(C\), multiplication is defined by the Koszul rule
\[ (b_1\otimes c_1)(b_2\otimes c_2)=(-1)^{\deg(c_1)\deg(b_2)}b_1b_2\otimes c_1c_2, \]
when the \(b_i\) and \(c_i\) are homogeneous.
If \((V_1,Q_1)\) and \((V_2,Q_2)\) are two quadratic modules, then \(C(Q_1\oplus Q_2)=C(Q_1)\otimes' C(Q_2)\) (Bourb. loc. cit.).

If \(P\) and \(Q\) are two \(A\)-modules which are locally free, \(\mathbb Z/2\)-graded and of finite type over \(A\), we identify the \(\mathbb Z/2\)-graded algebras \(\operatorname{End}(P)\otimes'\operatorname{End}(Q)\) and \(\operatorname{End}(P\otimes Q)\), by setting, for \(u,v,p\) and \(q\) homogeneous
\[ u\otimes v\,(p\otimes q)=(-1)^{\deg(v)\deg(p)}u(p)\otimes v(q). \]

Lemma 1.10. Let \((V,Q)\) be a quadratic module which is the sum of two quadratic modules \((V_1,Q_1)\) and \((V_2,Q_2)\), with \(V_i\) locally free of rank \(2m_i\) and \(Q_i\) non-degenerate. Let \(V_i=W_i\oplus W'_i\) be a decomposition of \(V_i\) into two totally singular subspaces \((i=1,2)\). Then the diagram
\[ \begin{array}{ccc} C(Q)&\xrightarrow{\sim}&C(Q_1)\otimes' C(Q_2)\\ \downarrow&&\downarrow\\ \operatorname{End}(\bigwedge(W_1\oplus W_2))&\xrightarrow{\sim}& \operatorname{End}(\bigwedge W_1\otimes\bigwedge W_2)\xrightarrow{\sim} \operatorname{End}(\bigwedge W_1)\otimes'\operatorname{End}(\bigwedge W_2) \end{array} \]

is commutative.
The verification is left to the reader. We deduce at once from this lemma that

1.11. The following construction is borrowed from Micali and Villamayor. If \(p:Z\longrightarrow S\) is a double étale covering of \(S\), we can regard \(Z\) as a \(\mathbb Z/2\)-torsor on \(S\). Let us denote by \(*\) the addition of torsors, and for \(s\) a section of \(Z\), let \(e(s)\) be the idempotent of \(p_*\mathcal O_Z\) which equals 1 on \(s\), and 0 elsewhere. With the notation of 1.10, there exists a unique isomorphism

\[ \tag{1.11.1}\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q_1))*\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q_2))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q)) \] \[ +:\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q_1))\times_S\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q_2))\longrightarrow\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q)) \]
for which, after any base change, we have
If \(s(W)\) is the section of \(\operatorname{Spec}(Z(Q))\) such that \(e(s(W))=e(W)\), the formula 1.10.1 can be rewritten

Proposition 1.12. Let \(A\) be a field, \(V\) a vector space of dimension \(2m>0\) over \(A\), \(Q\) a non-degenerate quadratic form on \(V\) and \(W_1,W_2\) two totally isotropic subspaces of dimension \(m\) of \(V\). Then, \(e(W_1)=e(W_2)\) if and only if \(\dim(W_1/W_1\cap W_2)\) is even.
(Cf. Bourbaki Alg. 9 § 6 ex. 28). Let \(e_1\ldots e_m\ f_1\ldots f_m\) be a basis of \(V\) such that

1. a) \(Q(\sum x_i e_i+\sum y_i f_i)=\sum x_i y_i\);
  2. b) \(W_1\) is generated by the \(e_i\);
  3. c) and \(W_2\) is generated by the \((e_i)_{1\leq i\leq s}\) and the \((f_i)_{s<i\leq m}\).
Then \(V\) is the sum of the \(V_i=Ae_i+Af_i\), and
\[ W_\alpha=\bigoplus W_\alpha\cap V_i\qquad(\alpha=1,2). \]

The addition formula 1.11 then allows us to restrict to the case where \(\dim V=2\). If \(W_1=W_2\), the assertion is obvious. If on the contrary \(W_1=Ae\), and \(W_2=Af\) with \(\Phi(e,f)=1\), we have \(e(W_1)=fe\) and \(e(W_2)=ef=1-fe\).
2. Quadrics

2.1. Let \(k\) be an algebraically closed field and \(V\) a vector space of dimension \(n+2\) over \(k\). A smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over \(k\) is a scheme \(X\) over \(k\) isomorphic to the subscheme of \(\mathbb P(V^*)\) defined by the equation \(Q=0\), for \(Q\) an ordinary quadratic form on \(k\).
Remark 2.2. For \(n=0,1,2\), \(X\) is a smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over \(k\) if and only if:
\[ \begin{array}{ccl} n=0&:&X\simeq\operatorname{Spec}(k)\amalg\operatorname{Spec}(k),\\ n=1&:&X\simeq\mathbb P_k^1,\\ n=2&:&X\simeq\mathbb P_k^1\times\mathbb P_k^1. \end{array} \]

Lemma 2.3. With the notation of 2.1, let \(X\) be defined by \(Q=0\).

(i) \(\Omega_X^n\simeq\mathcal O_X(-n)\).

(ii)

\[ \operatorname{Pic}(X)\simeq \begin{cases} 0&\text{for }n=0,\\ \mathbb Z&\text{for }n=1\ ;\ \mathcal O_X(1)\text{ is the square of a generator},\\ \mathbb Z\oplus\mathbb Z&\text{for }n=2,\\ \mathbb Z&\text{for }n\geq3\ ;\ \mathcal O_X(1)\text{ is a generator}. \end{cases} \]

(iii) \(H^i(X,\mathcal O_X)=0\) for \(i>0\), \(H^1(X,\mathcal O_X(1))=0\).

(iv) \(H^0(X,\mathcal O_X(1))\xleftarrow{\sim}H^0(\mathbb P(V^*),\mathcal O_V(1))=V^*\), and for \(n>0\), \(H^0(X,\mathcal O_X)=k\).

Assertion (i) follows from the fact that the conormal sheaf of \(X\) in \(\mathbb P(V^*)\) is \(\mathcal O_X(-2)\) and the canonical sheaf of \(\mathbb P^r\) is \(\mathcal O(-r-1)\). Asser- tion (ii) follows from 2.2 for \(n\leq2\), from SGA 2 XII 3.7 for \(n\geq3\). Finally (iii) and (iv) follow from FAC n° 78.
Definition 2.4. A smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over a scheme \(S\) is an \(S\)-scheme \(f:X\longrightarrow S\), proper and smooth over \(S\), whose geometric fibers are smooth quadrics.

For \(n=0\), a smooth quadric of dimension 0 over \(S\) is nothing other than a double étale covering of \(S\). For \(n=1\), it is nothing other than a Severi-Brauer variety of dimension 1 over \(S\).
2.5. If \(p:X\longrightarrow S\) is a smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over \(S\), then, by 2.3 (i), the inverse of the invertible sheaf \(\Omega^n_{X/S}\) is ample.

By 2.3 (ii), the Picard scheme \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) is smooth over \(S\) (since \(H^2(X,\mathcal O_X)=0\)), unramified over \(S\) (since \(H^1(X,\mathcal O_X)=0\)) and essentially proper over \(S\) (since \(X/S\) is smooth). It is therefore, locally on \(S\) for the étale topology, a constant discrete group having the structure indicated in 2.3 (ii).

In particular, if \(X\) has a section, there exists on \(X\) an invertible sheaf \(L\) such that \(L^{\otimes(-n)}\) has the same image in \(\operatorname{Pic}_{X/S}\) as \(\Omega^n_{X/S}\), and two such sheaves are isomorphic locally on \(S\). By 2.3 (iii) and [1] 0.5 p. 19, \(p_*L\) is locally free, of formation compatible with any base change. Consequently, \(p_*L\) is locally free of rank \(n+2\) (2.3 (iv)), the map from \(p^*p_*L\) to \(L\) is surjective, the canonical map from \(X\) to \(\mathbb P(p_*L)\) is a closed immersion, it identifies \(X\) with a relative divisor of degree 2 of \(\mathbb P(p_*L)\), and, locally on \(S\), \(X\) is the quadric of \(\mathbb P(p_*L)\) defined by an ordinary quadratic form on \((p_*L)^*\), unique up to an invertible factor.

2.6. Suppose \(n>0\). If \(L_1\) and \(L_2\) are two invertible sheaves of the type considered above, then, locally on \(S\), there exists an isomorphism \(u:L_1\xrightarrow{\sim}L_2\). This isomorphism is unique up to an invertible factor, since \(p_*\mathcal O_X=\mathcal O_S\) (by 2.3 (iv)). Consequently, the various isomorphisms \(u:L_1\xrightarrow{\sim}L_2\) induce the same isomorphism \(\mathbb P(u):\mathbb P(p_*L_1)\xrightarrow{\sim}\mathbb P(p_*L_2)\), so that \(\mathbb P(p_*L_1)\) is canonically isomorphic to \(\mathbb P(p_*L_2)\), and depends only on \(X/S\), not on the choice of an \(L\). We will denote it by \(P(X)\).

The projective space \(P(X)\) is defined locally on \(S\) for the étale topology. By descent, it defines a Severi-Brauer variety over \(S\), still denoted \(P(X)\), of which \(X\) is a relative divisor of degree 2.

Locally on \(S\), the pair \(X\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow P(X)\) defines a Clifford algebra \(C^+(X,P(X))\) (1.3). By descent, we obtain an algebra over \(S\), which we will simply denote by \(C^+(X)\).
2.7. Suppose that \(n=2m>0\). The center \(Z\) of \(C^+(X)\) is then a \(\mathcal O_S\)-algebra defining a double étale covering \(Z(X)\) of \(S\).
A generator of \(X\) is a subscheme \(D\) of \(X\) which is a linear subspace of dimension \(m\) of \(P(X)\).

Locally on \(S\), for \(P(X)\simeq\mathbb P(V^*)\) and \(X\) defined by the equation \(Q=0\), a generator \(D\) of \(X\) is identified by definition with a locally direct summand submodule \(W(D)\) of \(V\), totally singular and of rank \(m+1\). By 1.8, the idempotent \(e(W(D))\) of the center of \(C^+(X)\) depends only on \(D\subset X\). We denote by \(e(D)\) the corresponding section of \(Z(X)\) (the one on which \(e(W(D))\) equals 1).
The generators of \(X/S\) are the \(S\)-points of a scheme \(\operatorname{Gén}(X)\) projective over \(S\), and \(e\) defines a morphism of \(S\)-schemes
\[ \tag{2.7.1}e:\operatorname{Gén}(X)\longrightarrow Z(X). \]
When \(n=0\), we set \(Z(X)=X\), we call a generator of \(X\) a section of \(X\) and we define \(e\) to be the identity.
Proposition 2.8. Under the hypotheses of 2.7, \(p:\operatorname{Gén}(X)\longrightarrow S\) is pro- jective and smooth, and (2.7.1) is its Stein factorization.

The question is local for the étale topology on \(S\). Locally, if \(D\) is a generator, the generators \(D'\) disjoint from \(D\) are iden- tified, by 1.6, with the \(S\)-points of an affine space over a suitable \(S\), and one thus covers \(\operatorname{Gén}(X)\) by open sets smooth over \(S\).

To prove that the geometric fibers of \(e\) are geometri- cally connected, it suffices to consider the case where \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\), with \(X\) defined by the equation \(Q=0\) in \(\mathbb P(V^*)\).

Let then \(W_1\) and \(W_2\) be two totally isotropic subspaces of rank \(m+1\) of \(V\) such that \(e(W)=e(W')\), i.e. such that \(W/W\cap W'\) is of even dimension (1.12). Proceeding as in 1.12, we find an orthogonal decompo- sition
\[ V=V_0\oplus\bigoplus V_i \]
of \(V\) such that

1. a) \(W_\alpha=W_\alpha\cap V_0\oplus\bigoplus W_\alpha\cap V_i\quad(\alpha=1,2)\),
  2. b) \(W_1\cap V_0=W_2\cap V_0\),
  3. c) \(\dim V_i=4\), and \(V_i=(W_1\cap V_i)\oplus(W_2\cap V_i)\),

and it suffices to prove that \(W_1\cap V_i\) and \(W_2\cap V_i\) belong to one and the same connected family of maximal totally singular subspaces of \(V_i\). In other words, we are reduced to the case of quadrics of dimension 2. Such a quadric is isomorphic to \(\mathbb P^1\times\mathbb P^1\), the generators being the subschemes \(\{t\}\times\mathbb P^1\) and \(\mathbb P^1\times\{t\}\) ; the proposition is obvious in this case.
3. Cohomology of quadrics
3.1. Let \(p:X\longrightarrow S\) be a smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over \(S\), and \(\ell\) a prime number invertible on \(S\). We will denote by
\[ \eta\in H^0(S,R^2p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(1)) \]

the «cohomology class of a hyperplane section». Locally on \(S\) for the étale topology, we have \(P(X)\simeq\mathbb P(V^*)\), and \(\eta\) is then by definition the image in \(H^0(S,R^2p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\) of the first Chern class \(c_1(\mathcal O(1))\in H^2(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(1))\).

3.2. Suppose that \(n=2m\). A generator \(D\) of \(X\) then defines a cohomology class \(cl'(D)\) in \(H^n(X,\mathbb Z_\ell(m))\), with image \(cl(D)\) in \(H^0(S,R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m))\). It follows from 2.8 that the latter depends only on the section \(e(D)\) of \(Z(X)\), whence a morphism of étale sheaves on \(S\)
\[ \tag{3.2.1}cl:Z(X)\longrightarrow R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m). \]

Theorem 3.3. Let \(p:X\longrightarrow S\) be a smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over \(S\) and \(\ell\) a prime number invertible on \(S\).

(i) \(R^{2i+1}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell=0\).

(ii) If \(0\leq2i<n\), (resp. if \(n<2i\leq2n\)) then \(R^{2i}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(i)\) is canonically isomorphic to the constant sheaf \(\mathbb Z_\ell\), and is generated by \(\eta^i\) (resp. by \(\eta^i/2\)).

(iii) If \(n=2m\), the map deduced from (3.2.1)

\[ \tag{3.3.1}\mathbb Z_\ell^{Z(X)}\longrightarrow R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m) \]

is an isomorphism, and if \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are two disjoint sections of \(Z(X)\) (assumed isomorphic to \(S\amalg S\) ; this is locally the case), we have

\(a\) \(\eta^m=cl(\alpha)+cl(\beta)\),

\(b\)

\[ \begin{cases} \text{for }m\text{ even}:&\operatorname{Tr}(cl(\alpha)^2)=\operatorname{Tr}(cl(\beta)^2)=1,\quad cl(\alpha)cl(\beta)=0,\\ \text{for }m\text{ odd}:&cl(\alpha)^2=cl(\beta)^2=0,\quad \operatorname{Tr}(cl(\alpha)cl(\beta))=1, \end{cases} \]

\(c\) \(\eta(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta))=0\).
Assertions (i) and (ii) are included only for the record (XI 1.6, supplemented by XI 2.6 when \(2i+1=n\) in (i)).
It suffices to verify (iii) when \(S\) is the spectrum of an algebraically closed field \(k\) (it would even suffice to take \(S=\operatorname{Spec}(\mathbb C)\)).

Formula \(a\). We may assume that \(X\) is the quadric with equation \(\sum_0^m x_i x_{i+m+1}=0\) in \(\mathbb P^{2m+1}(k)\). The class \(\eta^m\) is the cohomology class of the plane section with equation
\[ x_i=0\quad(0\leq i<m), \]
and this cycle is the sum of the generators \(D_1\) and \(D_2\) with equation
\[ D_1:\ x_i=0\ (0\leq i\leq m),\qquad D_2:\ x_i=0\ (0\leq i<m)\text{ and }x_{2m+1}=0. \]
By 1.12, \(e(D_1)\neq e(D_2)\), so that \(\eta^m=cl(D_1)+cl(D_2)=cl(\alpha)+cl(\beta)\).

Formula \(b\). If generators \(D_1\) and \(D_2\) are disjoint (resp. meet transversally in one point) we have \(cl(D_1)cl(D_2)=0\) (resp. \(\operatorname{Tr}(cl(D_1)cl(D_2))=1\)). By 1.12, if \(m\) is even, we have \(e(D_1)\neq e(D_2)\) (resp. \(e(D_1)=e(D_2)\)) ; if \(m\) is odd, we have \(e(D_1)=e(D_2)\) (resp. \(e(D_1)\neq e(D_2)\)). The formulas \(b\) follow from this.

Formula \(c\). Obvious if \(m=0\). Otherwise, it suffices by (ii) to prove that \(\eta^m(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta))=0\). Indeed, \(\operatorname{Tr}(\eta^m(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta)))=\operatorname{Tr}(cl(\alpha)^2-cl(\beta)^2)=0\).
Verification.

\[ \begin{aligned} m\text{ even}:\quad 2=\operatorname{Tr}(\eta^{2m})&=\operatorname{Tr}(cl(\alpha)^2+2cl(\alpha)cl(\beta)+cl(\beta)^2)=1+1,\\ m\text{ odd}:\quad 2=\operatorname{Tr}(\eta^{2m})&=\operatorname{Tr}(cl(\alpha)^2+2cl(\alpha)cl(\beta)+cl(\beta)^2)=2. \end{aligned} \]
That 3.3.1 is an isomorphism now follows from the fact that the bilinear forms in two variables
\[ B(X,Y)=x_1y_2+x_2y_1\qquad\text{and}\qquad B(x,y)=x_1y_1+x_2y_2 \]
have discriminant \(\pm1\).
Verification 3.4. Let \(X\) be a smooth quadric of dimension \(n\) over a finite field \(\mathbb F_q\). Taking 3.3 into account, the Lefschetz trace formula gives the classical formula
\[ \#X(\mathbb F_q)= \begin{cases} \displaystyle\sum_0^n q^i&\text{for }n\text{ odd},\\ \displaystyle\sum_0^n q^i+\varepsilon q^m&\text{for }n=2m\quad(\varepsilon=\pm1). \end{cases} \]
For \(n\) even, we have \(\varepsilon=1\) if \(X\) is split, i.e. has a rational generator, and \(\varepsilon=-1\) otherwise.

3.5. If \(X\) is a smooth quadric over \(S\) of dimension \(n=2m\), we will call the primitive part of \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\) the orthogonal complement of \(\eta^m\) (with the notations of 3.3, this orthogonal complement is generated by \(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta)\)). We will call the primitive quotient of \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\) the quotient of \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\) by \(\mathbb Z_\ell\eta^m\). We define the primitive part and quotient of \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell\) by tensoring with \(\mathbb Z_\ell(-m)\).

3.6. Let \(V\) be a locally free vector bundle of rank \(n+2\) over \(S\) and \(X\) the smooth quadric over \(S\) defined by an ordinary quadratic form \(Q\) on \(V\). Let moreover \(H\) be a hyperplane of \(\mathbb P(V^*)\) which meets \(X\) transversally, and \(X^\circ=X-H\). The schemes \(X\) and \(Y=X\cap H\) are smooth quadrics over \(S\), of dimension \(n\) and \(n-1\). This makes it possible to use 3.3 to compute the cohomology of \(X^\circ=X-Y\).
\[ \tag{3.6.1} \begin{array}{ccccc} X^\circ&\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow&X&\longleftarrow\joinrel\lhook&Y\\ &\searrow f&\downarrow p&\swarrow q&\\ &&S&& \end{array} \]
If \(n=0\), then \(Y=\varnothing\) and \(X=X^\circ\). Suppose \(n>0\). We have two long exact sequences dual to each other

\[ \tag{3.6.2}\cdots\xrightarrow{\partial}R^if_!\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow R^ip_*\mathbb Z_\ell\xrightarrow{r_i}R^iq_*\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow\cdots \] \[ \tag{3.6.3}\cdots\xrightarrow{\partial}R^{i-2}q_!\mathbb Z_\ell(-1)\longrightarrow R^ip_*\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow R^if_*\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow\cdots. \]

For \(n\) even, the restriction homomorphism \(r_i\) is an isomorphism for \(i eq n,2n\) ; for \(n\) odd, it is an isomorphism for \(i eq n-1\). For \(n=2m\), and \(a\) the cohomology class of a generator, \(r_n(a)= rac12\eta^m\), so that \(r_n\) is surjective with kernel the primitive part of \(R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell\). For \(r=2m+1\), \(r_{2m}(\eta^m)=\eta^m\), so that \(r_{2m}\) is injective with cokernel the primitive quotient of \(R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell\). We deduce that \(R^if_!\mathbb Z_\ell=0\) for \(i eq n,2n\), and that \(R^if_!\mathbb Z_\ell\) is a free twisted constant \(\mathbb Z_\ell\)-sheaf of rank 1, for \(i=n,2n\). By duality, or using 3.5.3, one shows similarly that \(R^if_*\mathbb Z_\ell=0\) for \(i eq0,n\), that \(f_*\mathbb Z_\ell=\mathbb Z_\ell\), and that \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell\) is of rank one.
Suppose that \(n=2m\) and let us take up again the notations of 3.3.

\[ \begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longrightarrow&R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longrightarrow&R^nq_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&\downarrow\varphi&&\Vert&&&&\\ 0&\longleftarrow&R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longleftarrow&R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longleftarrow&R^{n-2}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m-1)&\longleftarrow&0. \end{array} \]
The sheaf \(R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\) then has two natural opposite generators \(\pm\delta\), with image \(\pm(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta))\) in \(R^np_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\). We have (3.3 (iii) b)
\[ \operatorname{Tr}(\delta^2)=(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta))^2=(-1)^m\cdot2 \]
and \(\pm\varphi(\delta)\) is the double of the natural dual generators \(\pm\delta'\) of \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\), namely the (opposite) images of \(cl(\alpha)\) and \(cl(\beta)\).
Suppose that \(n=2m+1\) and let us apply the notations of 3.3 to \(Y\).

\[ \begin{array}{ccccccccc} 0&\longrightarrow&R^{2m}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longrightarrow&R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\xrightarrow{\partial}&R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longrightarrow&0\\ &&&&&&\downarrow\varphi&&\\ R^{2m+2}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longleftarrow&R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m-1)&\longleftarrow&R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&\longleftarrow&0. \end{array} \]
The sheaf \(R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\) then has two natural opposite generators \(\pm\delta\) : \(\partial\,cl(\alpha)\) and \(\partial\,cl(\beta)\). We have
\[ \delta\cdot\varphi(\delta)=\delta^2=(\partial\,cl(\alpha))^2 =\partial(cl(\alpha)\cdot\partial\,cl(\alpha))=0 \]

so that \(\varphi(\delta)=0\). On the other hand, \(R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m+1)\) has two natural opposite generators \(\pm\delta'\), with image \(\pm(cl(\alpha)-cl(\beta))\) in \(R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\), and \(\operatorname{Tr}(\delta\cdot\delta')=\pm1\).
These results are collected in the following table
Table 3.7. Cohomology of affine quadrics of dimension \(n\), \(X^\circ=X-Y\).
A. The cohomology is torsion-free. The nonzero Betti numbers are:
\[ \begin{array}{ll} \text{cohomology without support}:&b_0=b_n=1\\ \text{cohomology with proper support}:&b_{2n}=b_n=1 \end{array} \]
B.

\[ \begin{array}{rcl} n=2m>0.&R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&=\text{primitive part of }R^{2m}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\\ &R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&=\text{primitive quotient of }R^{2m}p_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\\[4pt] n=2m+1&R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell(m)&=\text{primitive quotient of }R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m)\\ &R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m+1)&=\text{primitive part of }R^{2m}q_*\mathbb Z_\ell(m). \end{array} \]
These groups have, locally, natural generators defined up to sign, denoted \(\delta\) for the cohomology groups with proper support and \(\delta'\) for the others.
C. \(\varphi:R^nf_!\mathbb Z_\ell\longrightarrow R^nf_*\mathbb Z_\ell\) is zero for \(n\) odd, and sends \(\pm\delta\) to \(\pm2\delta'\) for \(n\) even \(>0\). We have
\[ \operatorname{Tr}(\delta\delta')=\pm1 \] \[ \operatorname{Tr}(\delta^2)= \begin{cases} 0&\text{if }n=2m+1\\ (-1)^m\cdot2&\text{if }n=2m. \end{cases} \]
Verification 3.8. The complex affine quadric \(S\) in \(\mathbb C^n\) with equation \(\sum z_i^2=1\) is diffeomorphic to the tangent bundle of the real sphere \(S\cap\mathbb R^n\).
This agrees with A and C.
BIBLIOGRAPHY
